translation: yakuza / organized crime member
naturalMeaning: Possibly from a losing hand in an old card game (8-9-3, ya-ku-za) — worthless, an outsider to normal society. 極道 and やーさん are common alternate/euphemistic terms.

translation: the outlaw/underworld life (a formal self-referential term)
naturalMeaning: Literally 'the extreme path' — a dramatic, almost formal way members refer to their own lifestyle in fiction, distinct from the more clinical/outsider term ヤクザ.

translation: an ordinary/law-abiding civilian (as opposed to a yakuza member)
naturalMeaning: The essential insider/outsider distinction in this world — 'don't drag a katagi into this' is a common protective line in fiction.

translation: boss (literally 'parent role')
naturalMeaning: Frames the boss-subordinate bond as a fictive parent-child relationship, formalized by the sakazuki ritual — not just a job title.

translation: the head of a gumi (organization/gang)
an older 'brother' figure in the organization (term of address/respect)
naturalMeaning: Used by a 舎弟 (shatei) to address someone above them who they've formed a sworn sibling-bond with via 盃 — not a literal blood brother.

translation: a sworn 'younger brother' in the organization
the second-in-command / lieutenant of a gumi
the sake-cup ritual that formalizes a fictive family bond
naturalMeaning: 盃を交わす (sakazuki o kawasu, 'to exchange the cup') creates a parent-child or sibling bond that isn't easily undone — the literal ceremonial foundation of the whole hierarchy vocabulary above.

translation: a hustle / racket / income-generating scheme
naturalMeaning: From 凌ぐ (shinogu, 'to endure/scrape by') — the day-to-day business/income side, whatever its legality, of the organization.

translation: the code of duty/honor (also a formal introduction ritual)
naturalMeaning: Borrowed from Confucian ethical vocabulary (benevolence + righteousness) but repurposed as the underworld's own honor code — also names the formal, almost scripted self-introduction ritual between members meeting for the first time.

translation: taking responsibility / drawing a line to make amends
naturalMeaning: ケジメをつける (kejime o tsukeru, 'to draw the line / settle it') — owning a mistake in a visible, formal way; in extreme genre fiction this is where the (real, historically-documented but rare) 指詰め (yubitsume, ritual fingertip removal) trope comes from.

translation: settling accounts / paying the price for a wrong
naturalMeaning: 落とし前をつけろ (otoshimae o tsukero, 'settle this') is a stock confrontation line — demanding someone make good on a debt or an insult.

translation: a turf war / gang conflict
a formal severing of ties (e.g. with a payout)
naturalMeaning: 手切れ金 (tegirekin) is the payout given when formally cutting ties with someone — ending a bond has its own vocabulary, mirroring how forming one (盃) does.

translation: traditional full-body tattoo
naturalMeaning: Strongly (though not exclusively) associated with yakuza membership in the popular imagination — real irezumi is a serious, painful, hand-poked traditional art form (also called 和彫り, wabori), not just decoration.

translation: ritual removal of a fingertip as an apology/penalty
naturalMeaning: A real, documented historical practice, though rarer today than fiction suggests — an extreme, visible form of taking ケジメ for a serious failure.

translation: I / me (rough masculine)
you (hostile/contemptuous)
naturalMeaning: Ironically began centuries ago as a respectful term of address (貴 'noble' + 様 honorific) before drifting all the way to an insult — a striking example of how politeness terms can flip meaning over generations.

translation: 'you bastard' / 'you son of a bitch'
naturalMeaning: 野郎 (yarou) alone is already a rough word for 'guy/bastard'; この野郎 as a set exclamation is one of the most stereotyped angry-confrontation lines in fiction.

translation: resolve / being mentally prepared (often for something bad)
naturalMeaning: 覚悟しろ (kakugo shiro, 'get ready' / 'prepare yourself') is a stock threat-line — telling someone to brace for consequences.

translation: to underestimate / look down on someone
naturalMeaning: Literally 'to lick', but 舐めるな (nameru na, 'don't underestimate me/us') or 舐めてんじゃねえぞ (a rough-contracted version) is a constant stock line in confrontation scenes.

translation: a full prostrating bow, forehead to the floor
naturalMeaning: The most extreme form of apology bow in Japanese culture generally, not yakuza-specific — but a common dramatic beat in this genre when someone is begging forgiveness or forcing a rival to submit.

translation: the organization's office/headquarters
naturalMeaning: A plain everyday word ('office') repurposed here as the specific term for a gumi's home base — context tells the two senses apart.

translation: an exaggerated rolled/trilled 'r' sound
naturalMeaning: A performative pronunciation exaggeration (not standard Japanese r) heavily associated with this fictional speech style — recognizing it helps you notice when media is deliberately signaling 'tough guy' through sound, not just word choice.
